title: Open Source Microfluidic Scaffolds
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"5k5c6","text":"There are no particular guidelines for this work.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"24i9a","text":"To produce a full microfluid mould, the user will need:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5mm19","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4krir","text":"Computer/Laptop (Windows or MacOS based)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bjclu","text":"An internet connection","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8nhol","text":"MEX/FDM 3D printer with suitable material (PLA recommended)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8gd05","text":"Hot plate/oven - capable of reaching 200  C","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":40,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":40,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"fgrkv","text":"Glass microscope slides (x2)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"31mkm","text":"Tweezers/tongs/oven gloves","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fkfsb","text":"Scalpel (or similar)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3lpte","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"60f9h","text":"Further equipment and material will be needed for PDMS casting (not discussed within this document).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"89qi8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"","data":{"formula":"\\degree","guid":"89AF94D0D27A11EA9651215F56FC09D9","id":0}}}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"818fq","text":"There are no particular safety guideliens required for this work. However, the following basic guidelines should be followed:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"epjfi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6gvr7","text":"MEX/FDM 3D printers should only be sued in well ventilated spaces and/or with a suitable filter","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3s89q","text":"Care should be taken when working with hot surfaces, with adequate signage warning a surface is hot if not immediately obvious","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"14er7","text":"Care should be taken whilst using sharp tools","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: User designs the required microfluid system from the sub-systems available.

Download, install and open Fusion 360. NB: Although Fusion 360 works on iOS and Android devices the add-in will not and so a Windows or MacOS device should be used. Fusion 360 Windows/MacOS/iOS/Android Autodesk
Load the µ-fluid add-in into Fusion 360
Download the µ-fluid add-in as a zip file from GitHub ( https://github.com/dmf-lab/microfluidics )

Choose module, set parameters and joints. You will have the option to "Export to stl" - this will export the generated model as an stl file (needed for printing) to a folder of the user's choice. The folder selection menu will appear after pressing "Ok".
Extract zip file to suitable location on your PC/Mac.

Within Autodesk Fusion 360 go to: "TOOLS -> ADD-INS -> Scripts and Add-Ins... -> Add-Ins" and click on the little "+" icon next to "My Add-Ins". Select the folder "Fusion Add-In" contained within the folder from the previous step.
Run the µ-fluid add-in. This will be shown in the "TOOLS" and "SOLID" toolbars.
Download, install and open Ultimaker Cura. Make sure to follow the correct set-up for your printer. Cura 4.3+ Windows/MacOS Ultimaker
Do you need more models? If so, go to step #4 . If not, continue.
Within Ultimaker Cura, open the model(s) generated previously. ("File -> Open File(s)...")

Change the profile as appropriate. NB: This will only work using the downloadable profiles when using Cura for an Ultimaker 3/3 Extended. If you would like to use a different printer, please open the appropriate profile and copy the relevant settings. This should give you a good starting point for printing the scaffolds.

The µ-fluid channel profiles can be downloaded from github: ( https://github.com/dmf-lab/microfluidics ). The profiles are contained within the "Cura Profiles" folder (within the Microfluidics zip). MicroChannels01.curaprofile - Intended for use with 0.1mm nozzle. MicroChannels04.curaprofile - Intended for use with 0.4mm nozzle.
Slice models on buildplate within Ultimaker Cura.
Save gcode to file and/or send gcode to printer.
Print models as you would a normal 3D print with the equipment available to you.

Once the print is completed, remove the models from the build plate. We found the best way to do this was using a stanley knife (or similar) and prising the connectors of the build plate. The channels are very delicate so this takes some practice and patience.
Mount the 3D-printed microfluid channel modules onto 1 mm thick glass microscope slides in the desired configuration using the ball-and-socket-connectors.
Place a second 1mm thick glass slide on top of the channel configuration to act as a weighting slide

Heat the slide-channel-slide assembly at 200 \degree C for 45-60s to bond the PLA thermoplastic to the glass substrate and smooth out inconsistencies. This is best performed on a temprature controlled hot plate, but heating could also be achieved in an oven or over a flame, however timings and cosistency will vary. CAUTION: Hot surfaces and heated assemblies can cause severse burns. NOTE: Heating times will vary depending on the height of the 3D-printed scaffold.
After heating, use tweerzers, tongs or oven gloves to transfer the thermally bonded slides onto a metal cooling plate (weighting slide down), and leave for 5 minutes.

Use the resulting thermally bonded channel design on the substrate slide as the master mould for PDMS casting. See work by Lake et al. for a more detailed explanation of this step ( https://doi.org/10.1038/protex.2015.069 ).

After cooling, slide a blade between the substrate and weighting slides and gently twist to remove the weighting slide. NOTE: If the channel scaffolds disbond slightly from the subrate during this step, they can be easily rebonded by breifly reheating the substrate slide at 200 deg C without the weighting slide.
creator: Harry Felton
